channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Hits the Stalemate Phase of His Interventions in Gaza, Ukraine and Now Iran
item.description: In Ukraine, Gaza and now Iran, President Trump’s early declarations of easy wins have given way to harsh reality.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: One of President Trump’s close aides said recently that destroying nuclear sites from the air is what America does best, and that controlling political events in nations like Iran, Russia and Ukraine is what the United States does worst.

item.title: U.S. Military Is Quietly Guiding Ships Through the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: U.S. Central Command has helped around 70 commercial ships pass through the strait in the last three weeks, an official said.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Vessels waiting to pass through the Strait of Hormuz. Before the U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran, well over 100 commercial ships a day passed through the strait.

item.title: Israel Captures Crusader Castle That Symbolized Its Long Lebanon Occupation
item.description: The seizure of Beaufort in southern Lebanon called up bitter memories in both countries amid a widening Israeli conflict with Hezbollah that seems far from over.
item.dc:creator: Aaron Boxerman and Natan Odenheimer
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A view of the Beaufort Castle fortification atop a cliff above the Litani River in southern Lebanon, last year.

item.title: Trump Administration Sees Striking Exodus of Legal Talent
item.description: The departure of more than 10,000 federal lawyers has left some agencies without sufficient staff and has boosted the ranks of state attorneys general offices and advocacy groups.
item.dc:creator: Eileen Sullivan and Andrea Fuller
item.media:credit: Rachel Woolf for The New York Times
item.media:description: Phil Weiser, the Colorado attorney general, with some of the 22 lawyers from across the federal government that he has hired since May of last year.

item.title: Trump Is Dominating GOP Primary Elections. Does That Mean Victory in the Midterms?
item.description: Come November, the Republican Party will need the support of voters outside of President Trump’s base, many of whom are deeply dissatisfied with the economy and the Iran war.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump heading to Trump National Golf Club in Sterling, Va., on Saturday. Mr. Trump’s preferred candidates have had strong showings in Republican primaries this year.

item.title: Release of Tina Peters, Election Denier and Former County Clerk, Divides Colorado City
item.description: The former clerk of Mesa County is scheduled to be set free on Monday, after her nine-year prison sentence was commuted by Gov. Jared Polis.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy
item.media:description: Tina Peters, a county clerk and election denier who was imprisoned for tampering with voting machines, in 2022.

item.title: Cory Booker Has ‘Concerns’ About Platner Revelations in Maine Senate Race
item.description: Mr. Booker and other Senate Democrats faced questions Sunday about reports that Mr. Platner’s wife previously told his campaign he had exchanged sexual messages with women outside his marriage.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times

item.title: He Was Satirized on British TV. Now He’s Trump’s Pick to Lead California.
item.description: Steve Hilton grabbed headlines when he worked in conservative politics in Britain. His American political renaissance in the California governor’s race has bemused former British colleagues and rivals.
item.dc:creator: Danny Hakim, Stephen Castle and Laurel Rosenhall

item.title: In U.F.O. Files, Some Christians See Vexing Questions — and Demons
item.description: The prospect of intelligent life elsewhere in the universe raises unsettling theological implications.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Graham
item.media:credit: U.S. Department of Defense, via Getty Images
item.media:description: The Pentagon recently released U.F.O. files that included murky videos and still images that did not show anything definitive.

item.title: Tilly Norwood, A.I. Actress, Wants to Know Why Everyone’s Mad at Her
item.description: The A.I. actress on her craft, the future of film and how she definitely does not intend to murder us.
item.dc:creator: Taffy Brodesser-Akner

item.title: India’s Hindu Right Has a New Hero: A 17th-Century Warrior King
item.description: Statues of Shivaji are rising everywhere. The founder of the Maratha empire who fought against the Mughal dynasty is now a symbol for nationalists remaking the country.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das, Suhasini Raj and Atul Loke
item.media:description: Visitors posing for selfies at the Shivaji statue in Malvan, a small town in Maharashtra, India, in January. There has been a surge of interest in the Hindu warrior in recent years.

item.title: The Sculptor Riding India’s Shivaji Craze
item.description: Demand has soared for his figurines and statues of the warrior king. It started with a video he posted online.
item.dc:creator: Anupreeta Das, Suhasini Raj and Atul Loke
item.media:description: Anant Ganpat Chougule working on a statue of the Maratha king Shivaji at his studio in Sawantwadi, a small town in Maharashtra, India, in January.

item.title: Havoc in Hawaii’s Coffee Fields After an Earthquake Destroyed Water Collection Tanks
item.description: An earthquake destroyed many water catchment systems on Hawaii’s Big Island, capping a spring filled with devastating floods.
item.dc:creator: Libby Leonard and Vivian Yee
item.media:credit: Megan Spelman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Roy Santana, 80, in front of his water catchment tank, which broke during a 6.0 earthquake earlier this month.

item.title: In Taiwan, ‘Mainland Spouses’ From China Become a Focus of Infiltration Fears
item.description: At the heart of a criminal case involving one such wife is a question: How can Taiwan protect its democracy without unfairly targeting Chinese migrants?
item.dc:creator: Chris Buckley and Amy Chang Chien
item.media:credit: Lam Yik Fei for The New York Times
item.media:description: A village near Taipei. Taiwan officials are confronting what they say is growing Chinese infiltration of the island’s politics, media and internet.

item.title: Joby Demonstrated its Air Taxi in Manhattan, but You Can’t Fly in It Yet
item.description: Aviation start-ups and the Trump administration want to replace helicopters with electric aircraft, but the new vehicles still have to pass arduous tests before the public can use them.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.media:description: Joby Aviation’s electric aircraft taking off from Kennedy International Airport for a flight to Manhattan in April as part of a federal program that aims to hasten the use of air taxis.

item.title: How Your Friend Group Influences How You Spend and Save
item.description: Our spending habits are often influenced by our social circles. You can help shape what it looks like.
item.dc:creator: Paulette Perhach
item.media:credit: Benjamin Norman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sandy Smith said she embraced being “cheap” because “I had to.” Now her friends come to her for financial advice.

item.title: The Museum of Wretched Ideas Has Reopened
item.description: We seem to have forgotten more or less everything.
item.dc:creator: David French
item.media:credit: Aleksey Kondratyev for The New York Times

item.title: Men and Women Should Be Optimists About Love, Not Heteropessimists
item.description: “Heteropessimism” is all the rage — but really, there’s never been a better time to be looking for love. Go on, be a hetero-optimist.
item.dc:creator: Magdalene J. Taylor

item.title: High-Speed Sailboats Race Past the New York Skyline
item.description: SailGP, a two-day competition some say is like Formula 1 on the water, has replaced part of the normal traffic in New York Harbor with high-tech catamarans.
item.dc:creator: David Waldstein
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: The U.S. Coast Guard cordoned off a race area in New York Harbor, which is usually crammed with vessels.

item.title: Notre Dame Students Abused by Priest in ‘Weighing Scheme,’ Report Finds
item.description: An investigation commissioned by Notre Dame found that the Catholic university could have done more to respond to allegations of sexual misconduct.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Saul
item.media:credit: Evan Jenkins for The New York Times
item.media:description: A new report details allegations of sexual abuse at the University of Notre Dame.

item.title: Israel Day Parade to Return to New York Against a Changed Backdrop
item.description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani did not attend the annual parade, which took place amid a deterioration of public support for Israel and rising antisemitism across the United States.
item.dc:creator: Liam Stack, Emma G. Fitzsimmons and Miles G. Cohen
item.media:description: Thousands of people gathered to celebrate at the Israel Day Parade on Fifth Avenue in Manhattan.

item.title: The World Capital of French Fries Has a Problem: Too Many Potatoes
item.description: Belgium’s potato harvest set a record, just as tariffs hit U.S. demand for frozen fries and as competition from suppliers in Asia intensified.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.media:credit: Kevin Faingnaert for The New York Times
